questions.text: I approach problems by trying things and seeing what works rather than defining them first.
I want to be part of shaping how a project unfolds, alongside others.
I experience the physical world more vividly when I'm in a heightened emotional state.
I believe that the moral significance of another person's pain should override considerations of personal cost.
I tend to frame requests as options rather than expectations.
I find that protecting my own interests helps me stay strong and effective.
I believe that what people say matters more than how they say it.
I plan vacations and trips well in advance, with itineraries and reservations.
I believe that the capacity to evaluate suffering without emotional contamination is what makes fair and scalable responses to human need possible.
I believe that when I process distressing situations through a lens of rational analysis, I am better able to protect the interests of all parties, not just the most vocal.
I believe that my emotional system has a built-in governor that keeps responses moderate.
I believe that a focused pursuit of personal excellence makes life meaningful.
I find meaning in being the person who gives the most and asks for the least.
I don't tend to experience the kind of emotional highs and lows that others describe.
I often establish the norms and expectations within a group.
I notice the difference between a genuine invitation and a courtesy one.
I prefer trips where I can wander freely and discover things without an itinerary.
I find that I am more interested in the logical structure of an argument than in its social context.
